turns.content: I'm trying to add a logger middleware to our Actix-web application but I thought I'd just wrap the handler with a simple function and return the result. It feels like I should be able to intercept requests easily without messing up the flow.

You're missing how actix-web handles middleware execution order. You can't just wrap handlers directly because request handling happens through a pipeline of components that follow specific rules. To add logging you need to use the Middleware trait and implement it so your logger gets called before and after each handler runs, ensuring everything in the chain executes correctly.
Okay, so I should be implementing something like an actix_middleware::Middleware? But how do I actually get access to the request and response objects inside that trait?

Yes, you implement the Middleware trait from actix_web which gives you access to the Request and Response types through its fields. You use these to read headers or modify body content as needed before passing them down the chain. The framework automatically calls your middleware implementation at the right points in the request lifecycle so you don't have to manually manage state between steps.

I'm trying to build a custom logger middleware for my Actix-web service, but I can't seem to get the request and response bodies through. I thought if I just implement the Message trait with an impl MyLogger for RequestBody and ResponseBody that wraps them in a String, then calls inner.into_bytes() inside on_message, it should work without changing how the framework handles data.

That's a common misconception; you can't just wrap the body types like that because RequestBody and ResponseBody don't implement the traits needed for middleware unless they're already part of the framework's internal state. The correct approach is to create a struct that implements the Middleware trait, then use actix_web::middleware::Logger or your own custom implementation by wrapping the handler with your middleware. You need to extract the raw bytes from the request before processing and ensure your middleware returns a new RequestBody that the Actix framework can still consume.

Ah, okay so I shouldn't be trying to wrap the body types directly. Can you show me how to write the actual struct for my logger? I want it to print a timestamped log entry whenever the request comes in and when the response is sent.

Sure, here's how you'd define your custom middleware: you'll create an actix_web::middleware::Logger or a similar struct that implements Middleware. Inside on_request and on_response methods, you call the framework's built-in logging hooks to get the request headers and status code, then format them with timestamps using chrono. You don't need to touch the actual body bytes in these callbacks because the middleware runs before the handler executes, so the data is already available at that stage.
Got it. So I just need to register my middleware with app.middleware(). MyLogger? And then how does this interact with other built-in middleware like Compression or Logger from the actix crate?

Yes, you chain your custom middleware using app.middleware(MyLogger). Your logger will run before or after depending on where you place it in the chain. It's independent of built-in middleware like Compression or Logger; they can coexist without conflict as long as each one implements Middleware correctly. You might also consider using actix-web's built-in Logger trait if you want to avoid writing your own, but customizing it gives you more control over formatting and scope.
Thanks! One last thing though—what happens if someone tries to use this middleware with async handlers that have side effects? Will the logging still happen even if the handler panics?

The logging will only occur up until the point where the panic happens. If your handler panics before returning a response, your on_response callback won't fire because there's no successful response to send. However, you can wrap your middleware in an error handler using actix_web::middleware::Logger or similar tools that catch errors and log them separately so you always get visibility into failures too.
